sections.recommendation:
Adopt a Resolution authorizing the City Manager or designee to reallocate Youth Equity
Partnership (YEP) funds awarded through the FY 2025 - 2028 community agency
contracting process to the Berkeley Public Schools Fund, Contract No. 32500058, and
amend the Schools Fund’s existing contract as follows:
 Remove the Black Girls United (BGU) program from the contract (at the
contractor’s request).
 Reallocate $20,000 of the BGU funding to the Longfellow Identity Affirming
Programs (LIAP) for FY 2026 through FY 2028, increasing their total annual
allocation from $25,300 to $45,300.
 Return the remaining $27,817 initially awarded to BGU, annually, to the General
Fund for FY 2026 - 2028.
sections.fiscal_impacts:
Contract #32500058 for the Berkeley Public Schools Fund is part of the Berkeley Youth
Equity Partnership’s (YEP) Healthy, Connected, and Resilient portfolio. The Schools
Fund acts as a fiscal intermediary for these youth programs. The funds are designated
to promote the mental health, well-being, and sense of belonging of African
American/Black and Latinx young people in Berkeley.
BGU’s Manager advised the City that the program is changing strategies, making it no
longer eligible for fiscal sponsorship from the Schools Fund or for funding from YEP.
This recommendation would shift $20,000 per year in funding from FY 2026 through FY
2028 within the City’s existing contract with the Schools Fund, moving funds from BGU
to LIAP. Staff determined that this reallocation is consistent with the terms of the FY
2025 RFP, because this shift would occur within an existing contract (with the Schools
Fund) and would remain under the same YEP priority (Healthy, Connected, and
Resilient) as originally awarded by Council.
2180 Milvia Street, Berkeley, CA 94704 ● Tel: (510) 981-7000 ● TDD: (510) 981-6903 ● Fax: (510) 981-7099
E-Mail: manager@CityofBerkeley.info Website: http://www.CityofBerkeley.info/Manager

If approved, the total not-to-exceed amount of the Schools Fund contract would
decrease by $83,451 (from $292,468 to $209,017). This $83,451 represents the total
funds removed from the Black Girls United program's allocation for FY 2026 through FY
2028 that will be returned to the General Fund.
sections.current_situation:
On June 25, 2024, the Berkeley City Council adopted the FY 2025 budget, which
included authorizing the City Manager or designee to execute a contract with the
Berkeley Public Schools Fund for a total not-to-exceed amount of $292,468 ($73,117
per year for FY 2025 through FY 2028) to fund two new programs: BGU and LIAP.
In its first year with YEP funding, BGU struggled to build its administrative capacity,
maintain staffing, and meet enrollment/participation goals. In May 2025, YEP received
notification from the BGU Manager that its leadership decided to shift its programmatic
focus away from in-school programming. BGU subsequently requested to conclude
participation in the current grant after FY 2025.
During the first half of FY 2025, LIAP served 165 Black and Brown middle school
students. Approximately 77% (127 students) of these participants were Latinx, and 23%
(38 students) were African American/Black.
LIAP’s Raza Youth Consilio (RYCO) component includes a biweekly community-
building group focused on students of Latinx heritage, though the curriculum is relevant
to participants of all racial/ethnic backgrounds. RYCO engages youth in assets-based
identity-awareness activities, focusing on beliefs, values, purpose, assets, cultural roots,
and family interaction. A subset of RYCO’s students comprise a leadership team that
meets bi-weekly to plan and support the larger group sessions.
The second component, Young MENtors, provides intervention services specifically
designed for boys of color. LIAP’s RJ Counselor works with a team of Longfellow
teachers and leaders to provide group and one-on-one support for 45 high-need
students in areas including attendance, emotional regulation, and academics.
In its FY 2025 proposal to the City, LIAP requested a modest budget of $25,300. As
first-time applicants for YEP funding, the Schools Fund and LIAP staff did not fully
anticipate the time and staff expertise needed to collect service measures and
outcomes, and to track attend
sections.rationale:
A key finding of Berkeley Unified School District’s (BUSD) Mental Health Needs
Assessment 2023[1] was that “…families of color resoundingly shared that services and
supports have not been focused toward honoring the diversity of student backgrounds
Page 2
Page 3 of 5
Reallocation of FY 2025 Youth Resiliency Funding CONSENT CALENDAR
July 22, 2025
and experiences; as a result, a culture of exclusion and inequity persists.” The
assessment recommended “[a] focused approach…[to address] racial disparities
experienced by students and families…”
The YEP funding awarded to the Schools Fund supports this recommended approach
by investing in programs and services that promote resiliency, positive cultural identity,
and belonging. With BGU stepping away from this contract, the City has an opportunity
to bolster its sister program, LIAP, by providing additional funding. This will allow LIAP
to build its administrative capacity and maintain program quality in the face of growing
enrollment. The increase in funding will help ensure that African American/Black and
Latinx students at Longfellow Middle School continue to experience the benefits of this
program: stronger cultural identity, increased connectedness, and greater resiliency.
ENVIRONMENTAL SUSTAINABILITY AND CLIMATE IMPACTS
There are no identifiable environmental effects, climate impacts, or sustainability
opportunities associated with the subject of this report.
sections.alternatives:
Staff consulted with the Schools Fund to try to identify a solution that would enable BGU
to continue to receive YEP funding. BGU opted to end their participation in the contract.
Staff considered the benefits of the City retaining the full balance of the BGU award to
help address its deficit. Staff balanced this option with the City’s commitment to support
vulnerable populations, including those impacted by recent federal immigration policies
and practices. Particularly in light of these policy changes, LIAP plays an important role
by providing Latinx middle schoolers and their families with a counterbalance to and
refuge from the negative messaging and actions occurring across the country.
If it is not awarded the proposed additional funding, LIAP would need to divert a portion
of its current grant award to support the program’s administration. This shift would result
in the need to dilute program services and/or to turn away participants. In contrast,
increasing LIAP’s allocation by $20,000 per year for FY 2026 – FY 2028 would enable
LIAP to support a fast-growing participant pool and to build infrastructure to collect
participant information, track expenditures, and document program impacts.
Given these considerations, staff recommends that $47,817 in funding originally
awarded to support the BGU program be reallocated in FY 2026 – 2028 to increase
LIAP’s annual funding by $20,000 (from $25,300 to $45,300) and the remaining $27,817
per year be returned to General Fund for FY 2026 – 2028.
CONTACT PERSON
Nina Goldman, Senior Management Analyst, HHCS, 510-981-5420

RESOLUTION NO. ##,###-N.S.
REALLOCATION OF FY 2025 YOUTH RESILIENCY FUNDING
WHEREAS, on June 25, 2024, City Council approved Berkeley’s FY 2025-2028
community agency contracts as part of the FY 2025 budget; and
WHEREAS, the approved budget included an annual allocation of $73,117 in General
Funds for FY 2025 thru FY 2028 to the Berkeley Public Schools Fund, fiscal sponsor for
Black Girls United ($47,817/year) and Longfellow Identity Affirming Programs ($25,300)
for a total not to exceed amount of $292,468 under Contract #32500058; and
WHEREAS, both programs were awarded funding to advance the Youth Equity
Partnership’s Healthy, Connected and Resilient priority; and
WHEREAS, in May 2025, Black Girls United notified the Youth Equity Partnership that it
was changing its programmatic approach and had decided to withdraw from its YEP
funding award effective the end of FY 2025; and
WHEREAS, Berkeley Public Schools Fund’s other sponsored program, Longfellow
Identity Affirming Programs, experienced enrollment growth in FY 2025 and recognized
that its contract required administrative obligations beyond what had been budgeted; and
WHEREAS, reallocating $20,000 annually in funding from Black Girls United to
Longfellow Identity Affirming Programs (within the Berkeley Public Schools Fund Contract
#32500058) would help strengthen the administrative infrastructure of Longfellow Identity
Affirming Programs; and
WHEREAS, the remaining funds in the amount of $83,451 ($27,817/year in FY 2026, FY
2027, and FY 2028) would be reabsorbed by the City’s Fund 011 General Fund.
NOW THEREFORE, BE IT RESOLVED by the Council of the City of Berkeley that the
City Manager or designee is authorized to amend its contract with the Berkeley Public
Schools Fund, Contract #32500058, as follows: reallocate $20,000 annually of funding
awarded to the Berkeley Public Schools Fund as fiscal sponsor from Black Girls United
to the Longfellow Identity Affirming Programs (increas
